content.problemStatement:
Sereja has an undirected graph on **N** vertices. There are edges between all but **M** pairs of vertices.


A permutation **p** on the vertices of the graph is represented as **p[1], p[2], … , p[N]** such that for all **i**, **p[i]** is a vertex of the graph. A permutation is called connected if there is an edge between vertices **p[i]** and **p[i+1]** for all natural numbers **i** less than **N**. Sereja wants to know the number of connected permutations on the graph vertices.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, denoting the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and M, where N is the number of vertices and M is the number of pairs of vertices that are not connected by an edge. The next M lines contain pairs of indices of vertices that are not connected by an edge.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of connected permutations on the graph vertices modulo 10^9 + 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there are two connected permutations: [1, 3, 2, 4] and [4, 2, 3, 1]. For the second test case, there are no connected permutations.
For this test case, there are 5! = 120 connected permutations since all vertices are connected.
For this test case, there are no connected permutations since the only pair of vertices is not connected.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a brute force approach by generating all possible permutations of the vertices and checking if each permutation is connected. A permutation is connected if there is an edge between each pair of adjacent vertices in the permutation. We can use a recursive function to generate all permutations and check if each permutation is connected.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N! * M) per test case, O(T * N! * M) total. The time complexity is high due to the brute force approach.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the recursion stack.
